Plaintiff Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order To Limit Defendant 4 Usa Loans, Inc. Dba Premier Realty Associates To 35 Special Interrogatories Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; Declaration Of Michael D. Lane
Off Calendar Oct-20-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Michael Hogue (hoguem@gtlaw.com) of Greenberg Traurig, LLP for Plaintiffs.
No other appearances.

Discussed. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFFs MING CAI and WEI FANG's Motion For Protective Order To Limit Defendant 4 Usa Loans, Inc. Dba Premier Realty Associates To 35 Special Interrogatories:

According to the verified Complaint: plaintiffs Ming Cai and Wei Fang are citizens and residents of China with a limited ability to speak and read the English language; defendant 4 USA Loans, Inc. dba Premier Realty Associates ("Premier") is a licensed real estate brokerage; defendant Min Zhao ("Zhao") is a real estate sales licensee for whom Premier was the supervising broker; and defendant Keith Nesbitt ("Nesbitt") is an associate of Zhao. The verified Complaint further alleges that Zhao obtained a power of attorney from plaintiffs to complete the purchase of a condominium in San Francisco and used it without authorization to convey an interest in plaintiffs' condominium to Nesbitt and otherwise took advantage of plaintiffs. Premier has propounded 524 special interrogatories to plaintiffs and plaintiffs have moved for a protective order limiting Premier to 35 special interrogatories to each plaintiff.

The court is directed by the Discovery Act to "limit the scope of discovery if it determines that the burden, expense, or intrusiveness of that discovery clearly outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The court may make this determination pursuant to a motion for protective order by a party or other affected person." Code Civ. Proc sec. 2017.020 (a). Presumptively, no party may propound more than 35 special interrogatories to any other party. Code Civ. Proc. sec. 2030.030(a)(1). Premier's special interrogatories exceeded the 35 question limit of section 2030.030 by 227 to each plaintiff for a total excess of 454.

A propounding party can exceed the 35 question limit by submitting a code-compliant declaration under Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.050. Section 2030.050 requires the necessary declaration to "state each factor described in Section 2030.040 that is relied on, as well as the reasons why any factor relied on is applicable to the instant lawsuit." Premier submitted a declaration reciting one of the factors described in Section 2030.040 ("because of the complexity and quantity of the existing potential (sic) issues . . . ") but failed to state the reasons why the factor relied upon is applicable to the instant lawsuit as required. Accordingly, the court finds that defendant has failed to submit a code-compliant declaration under Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.050 and therefore failed to justify exceeding the 35 question limit imposed by section 2030.030.
Moreover, the court finds that, even if the declaration were code-compliant, it is insufficient because the Complaint does not present a quantity or complexity of issues which would justify 262 special interrogatories to each plaintiff. On the contrary, the Complaint is a mere 16 pages long, contains numerous repetitions and deals chiefly with the alleged course of dealing by Zhao and Nesbitt. Premier has failed to carry its burden of justifying the number of special interrogatories propounded. Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2030.040(b).

Finally, the court finds that the burden, expense, and intrusiveness of the discovery clearly outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Code Civ. Proc sec. 2017.020 (a).

Therefore, plaintiffs' motion to limit Premier to 35 special interrogatories to each plaintiff is granted.

Sanctions were not requested and for that reason will not be granted.

Court to review the proposed order that has been submitted by the previling party.

Judge Pro Tem: James Fleming, Clerk: S. Le, Not reported. =(302/JPT)

Mr. Raidsnider argued that Keith should be able to defend himself in this action, that Keith was not obigated to inform the plaintiff that he moved and that Keith acted promptly when the default was entered.

The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KEITH NESBITT, BIG BLOCK CONSULTING, INC. Notice Of Motion To Set Aside Defaults is GRANTED. Defendants to file Answers forthwith. Prevailing party to prepare order in compliance with CRC3.1312(b). Judge Charles F. Haines, Clerk: N. Wong, Not reported (D501)
